summary: Superficial fascia contains fat and cutaneous nerves, while deep fascia wraps around muscles.
Deep fascia contains fat and superficial veins, while superficial fascia wraps muscles.
Both fascial layers are identical in structure and function.
The arm is divided into compartments by medial and lateral intermuscular septa.
The arm is divided into compartments by the interosseous membrane.
The arm has three compartments separated by three intermuscular septa.
Compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy to relieve pressure.
Compartment syndrome is treated conservatively with elevation and ice.
Compartment syndrome only occurs in the lower limb, never the upper limb.
The cephalic vein runs on the lateral side and the basilic vein on the medial side of the upper limb.
The cephalic vein runs on the medial side and the basilic vein on the lateral side.
The cephalic and basilic veins both drain into the external jugular vein.
The median cubital vein connects the cephalic and basilic veins at the cubital fossa.
The median cubital vein connects the cephalic vein to the brachial vein.
The median cubital vein is a deep vein accompanying the brachial artery.
The shoulder joint is a ball-and-socket joint with the greatest range of motion but prone to dislocation.
The shoulder joint is a hinge joint that allows flexion and extension only.
The shoulder joint is the most stable joint due to strong ligamentous support.
